title: Rally projects & scoping containers
topic: Rally (CA / Broadcom Agile Central) — what is a 'Project,' and how does it scope users, work, and visibility? Comparative pass across Jira, Azure DevOps, Aha!, Linear.
summary: Rally Project = permission + work + team container nested under Workspace; PortfolioItems float at Workspace level so strategy spans Projects.
content:
Contents
  1. Summary
  2. Key Findings
  3. Detailed Analysis
  4. Comparative View — Rally vs Jira vs Azure DevOps vs Aha! vs Linear
  5. Sources
  6. Gaps & Limitations

Summary

In Rally (CA / Broadcom Agile Central) a Project is the load-bearing scoping container: it is simultaneously a permission boundary, a work-item container (stories, defects, tasks, iterations, releases), and an organisational stand-in for a delivery team. Projects nest via a single Parent reference into an unlimited-depth hierarchy under a Workspace (which itself sits under a Subscription); a user holds independent role assignments per Project (Viewer, Editor, Project Admin) plus higher-level Workspace Admin / Subscription Admin roles, and Portfolio Items deliberately float at the Workspace level above the Project hierarchy so a single strategy item can have children scattered across many Projects [1][2][3][4].

Key Findings

  • Project = team + permission boundary + work container, all at once. Broadcom states a Project “just means a node of your organizational structure which represents a team” and is the container under which user stories, defects, tasks, iterations, and releases live [1][7].
  • Hierarchy is Subscription → Workspace → Project → (child Project)*. Workspaces are siloed (no cross-workspace data); Projects nest beneath a Workspace through a Parent reference at unbounded depth [1][3][6].
  • The parent-child link lives on the Project entity itself as the Parent Team field, populated automatically when a child is created via the “Add Child” flow [3].
  • Three Project-scope roles plus a Team Member modifier. No Access, Viewer, Editor, Team Member (Editor + visibility boost), and Project Admin; Workspace adds User / Workspace Admin; Subscription adds Subscription Admin [2][4].
  • Multi-project membership is the norm. Users hold independent ProjectPermission rows per Project, and TeamMembership can attach a user to many Projects in one call [4][2].
  • Portfolio Items are administered at the Workspace level, not the Project level. Their children (stories, features) can live in any Project under that Workspace, deliberately decoupling strategy from team boundaries [5][7].
  • Iterations and Releases are owned by Projects (timeboxes per Project). Releases at the top of a hierarchy can “cascade” into descendant Projects so a programme-level release timebox appears in every team’s view [8].
  • Projects cannot be deleted, only Closed. Closed Projects lock work items, drop out of pickers, and remain searchable; child Projects must be closed before parents; the only edit allowed on locked items is changing the project assignment [9].
  • Cross-Project work is supported within a Workspace. Children of a Portfolio Item can sit in any Project; users see whichever Projects they have permission on; the project picker is the UI scope switcher [5][7].
  • The term “Project” is the published name but it is interchangeable with “Team” in Broadcom’s own docs (“parent Team”, “child Team” appear on the create-child-project form). Rally has no separate Team entity at the data-model level — TeamMembership is just bulk ProjectPermission assignment [3][4].

Detailed Analysis

1. Definition: what a Rally Project actually is

Broadcom’s “Understanding the Rally Project Hierarchy” page is unusually direct: “a project in Rally just means a node of your organizational structure which represents a team” [1]. The Project is therefore not a project-management artefact (a bounded initiative with a start/end), but a long-lived container that simultaneously plays three roles:

  • Permission boundary — access is granted per Project via ProjectPermission [4].
  • Work container — user stories, defects, tasks, iterations, and releases are scoped to a Project [1][8].
  • Team stand-in — the same docs use “parent project” and “parent team” interchangeably; the create-child-project form labels the field as “Parent Team” [3][1].

Releases and iterations (Rally’s “timeboxes”) are also scoped to a Project: “The iteration artifact is unique for each Rally project” [8].

2. Hierarchy: Subscription → Workspace → Project → (child Project)*

Three nested containers exist in Rally [1][6][7]:

  1. Subscription — the tenant; one per customer.
  2. Workspace — data silo; “You cannot share a portfolio or its contents across workspaces” [5]. Multiple workspaces per subscription are permitted but Broadcom recommends limiting them.
  3. Project — nests under a Workspace; can have child Projects to unbounded depth.

The parent-child link is a single Parent Team reference on the Project itself: “The Parent Team field exists on the project object and is automatically populated with the parent project name during child project creation” [3]. This is the same single-FK pattern used by PortfolioItem — one nullable parent reference, no multi-parenting. The documentation does not specify a hard depth limit; Broadcom describes “multi-level” hierarchies and recommends one node at the top representing the company/division [1].

Child projects inherit selectively from the parent at create time [3]:

  • Releases and iterations — copied by default.
  • Milestones — copied by default.
  • User permissions — not copied by default (opt-in).
  • Shared custom pages — optional.

Note this is a copy at creation, not a live cascade — permission inheritance from parent to child is not the default model (see §4).

3. What lives inside a Project

Concretely scoped to a Project:

  • User Stories (HierarchicalRequirement) [1][7]
  • Defects [1]
  • Tasks (children of stories) [1]
  • Test Cases (via the parent story’s project) [implicit, follows story]
  • Iterations — “The iteration artifact is unique for each Rally project” [8]
  • Releases — per Project, with optional cascade to descendants [8]
  • Milestones — per Project [3]

Concretely not scoped to a Project (scoped to the Workspace instead):

  • Portfolio Items (PortfolioItem/Theme, PortfolioItem/Initiative, PortfolioItem/Feature) — “Your portfolio is administered at the workspace level” [5]. Their children (the stories/features that roll up to them) can live in any Project of the Workspace: “The children of a portfolio item do not need to be stored in the same project, nor a child project of the portfolio item itself” [7].

This is the load-bearing fact for the user’s design question: in Rally, strategy floats at the Workspace level and execution is bucketed into Projects. The two hierarchies are deliberately orthogonal.

4. Membership and permissions

Rally exposes a three-tier permission model with named roles at each tier [2][4]:

ScopeRolesNotes
Subscription Subscription Admin Highest authority; cannot be edited by Workspace Admin.
Workspace No Access, User, Workspace Admin Workspace User is the baseline that lets you receive Project permissions.
Project No Access, Viewer, Editor, Team Member, Project Admin Team Member = Editor with extra UX (owner dropdowns, team status pages).

Adding a user to a Project automatically grants them Workspace User on the parent Workspace [2]. A user can hold different roles on different Projects within the same Workspace, and TeamMembership is essentially a bulk-add of ProjectPermission rows for many Projects at once [4]. There is no separate Team entity at the data-model level — the “team” vocabulary is naming, not structure.

The fetched docs do not describe automatic cascade of Project permissions from parent to child; the create-child-project form explicitly offers “Copy all Users with existing permissions from the parent Team” as an opt-in checkbox, disabled by default [3]. So inheritance is by-copy-at-creation, not by-live-cascade.

5. Cross-Project work and visibility

  • A PortfolioItem in Project-agnostic Workspace space can have child stories scattered across any Project the user has access to [5][7].
  • Cross-project dependencies between work items require Edit rights on both projects: “Teams will also need edit rights if they need to create dependencies between work items across projects” [5].
  • Filter/Work Views can include items across multiple Projects, and these views can be saved and shared [5].
  • The UI scope switcher is the “project picker”; closed Projects drop out of it but remain searchable via global search and direct URL [9].
  • WSAPI queries default to the user’s default Workspace; the workspace= query param overrides scope; project= scopes to a Project (and parent/child traversal flags optionally include ancestors/descendants) [10].

6. Project vs Team vs Iteration

Rally does not have a separate first-class Team entity. The “team” concept is naming-only: a Project “represents a team” [1], the parent reference is labelled “Parent Team” [3], and TeamMembership in WSAPI is bulk Project assignment [4]. There is no notion of a long-lived Team that spans Projects; if you want one team to work across multiple Projects, you grant the user Editor on each Project individually.

Iterations sit under a Project. Each Project owns its own iteration timeboxes; the same calendar iteration in two Projects is two distinct Iteration objects (this is why the “copy iterations from parent” checkbox exists at child create time) [3][8]. Releases work the same way but support a “cascade” mechanism so a top-of-hierarchy Project can push the same Release into all descendants for cross-team rollup [8].

Rally has no entity for SAFe-style ART (Agile Release Train) or Value Stream at the data-model level. Programme structure is expressed by the Project hierarchy itself — e.g. an ART becomes a parent Project with team Projects as children. (This is borne out by the Broadcom recommendation to “always add a node at the top of your hierarchy that represents your company or division” [1].)

7. Lifecycle

  • Delete: not supported. “You cannot delete workspaces or projects” [9].
  • Close (= archive): Project transitions to Closed via Setup → Workspaces & Projects → Details → State field [9].
  • Effects of Close: work items locked; not editable except to change project assignment; not in pickers; still searchable; still rolling up into portfolio totals [9].
  • Order of operations: child Projects must close before parents; all Projects must close before a Workspace can close [9].
  • Reopen: requires parent Project and Workspace to be open first [9].
  • Move work between Projects: the close-projects page documents that “changing the project” is the one allowed edit on locked work items, implying Project reassignment is supported via the work item’s Project field; explicit cross-project move flows were not surfaced in the fetched pages [9].
  • Recycle Bin: deleted work items (not Projects) go to a recycle bin from which they can be restored [9].

8. Has the term evolved?

“Project” remains the published primary term across current Broadcom TechDocs — the navigation path is “Set up your projects/teams”, the API entity is Project, and permission entities are ProjectPermission [1][2][3][4]. However, the docs frequently shadow it with “Team”: the parent reference is “Parent Team”, the create wizard calls iterations “Releases and iterations from the parent Team”, and the prose treats the two as synonyms [1][3]. ValueOps (Broadcom’s umbrella branding for Rally + Clarity) introduces “Value Stream” vocabulary at the cross-product layer, but at the Rally data-model level there is no Value Stream or ART entity — those concepts continue to be expressed through the Project hierarchy.

Comparative View — Rally vs Jira vs Azure DevOps vs Aha! vs Linear

Question Rally Jira Azure DevOps Aha! Roadmaps Linear
Top-level scoping container name Project (under Workspace, under Subscription) [1][6] Project; flavoured as team-managed or company-managed [11] Project (under Organization); “fundamental container for storing data and source code” [12] Workspace (deliberately not “Project”); types include Product, IT, Project, Marketing, Services [13] Team (under Workspace); Project is a separate, time-bounded concept [14]
What it scopes (work items, perms, settings) Stories, defects, tasks, iterations, releases, permissions; PortfolioItems sit at Workspace level above Projects [1][5] All issues, workflows, schemes; team-managed = self-contained config; company-managed = shared schemes across Projects [11] Work items, repos, pipelines, test plans, wikis; one Project is large and isolating — cross-project linking loses history [12] Records (features/epics/initiatives), strategy, releases per Workspace; terminology is per-type (e.g. “feature” in Product workspaces, “activity” in Project workspaces) [13] Issues, Cycles, settings live in Team; Projects/Initiatives float across Teams [14]
Relation to teams / users Project ≈ team (no separate Team entity); per-Project role assignment; user can be in many Projects [1][2][4] Project membership via roles + permission schemes; users on many projects normal [11] Project auto-creates a same-named Team; Teams own Area Paths within a Project; one user, many teams [12][15] Workspace owners + custom user roles; users on many workspaces [13] Team has persistent membership; Projects can be shared across Teams [14]
Does it nest? Yes — Parent on Project, unbounded depth [3] Projects do not nest; cross-project hierarchy via Plans/Advanced Roadmaps [11] Projects do not nest; intra-Project depth via Area Paths up to 14 levels [15] Yes — Workspace lines nest Workspaces (company → division → product line → product) [13] Teams do not nest; Initiatives → Projects → Issues acts as a separate nesting axis [14]
Strategy items above the container PortfolioItems live at Workspace, span Projects [5][7] Plans / cross-project hierarchy in Premium tier [11] Cross-project work hard; recommended pattern is one Project + Area Paths [12] Initiatives + Goals span Workspaces via Workspace lines [13] Initiatives sit above Projects, span Teams [14]
Lifecycle (delete?) Cannot delete; Close locks work items but keeps them searchable [9] Archive/delete supported; team-managed projects can be deleted [11] Delete supported; Restore via recycle bin [12] Workspaces can be archived/deleted by admins [13] Teams can be archived [14]

Notable design decisions per tool

  • Aha!’s rejection of “Project” as the top-level term is deliberate and documented: their primary scoping unit is the Workspace, with workspace lines as the nesting axis. They reserve “Project” for one specific workspace type (alongside Product, IT, Marketing, Services), explicitly to avoid the connotation that all work is bounded-by-deadline [13].
  • Linear’s split of Team and Project is the cleanest distinction in this set: Team = persistent group with members and configuration; Project = bounded, time-boxed initiative that can be shared across Teams; Initiative = company-level grouping above Projects. This explicitly separates ownership from initiative-bounding [14].
  • Azure DevOps’s heavyweight Project is famously sticky: cross-project links lose history, so Microsoft recommends a single Project with Area Paths (up to 14 levels) for sub-structure rather than multiple Projects [12][15].
  • Jira’s team-managed vs company-managed split isn’t about hierarchy at all — it’s about whether configuration (workflows, schemes) is local to a Project or shared across Projects. Both flavours sit at the same hierarchy level [11].
  • Rally’s deliberate decoupling of strategy (Workspace-level Portfolio) from execution (Project-level work) is the architectural move worth singling out: it lets a single strategic item have children scattered across many delivery teams without forcing the strategy to live inside any one team’s Project [5][7].

  5. Creating Portfolio Items — Broadcom TechDocs
  6. How workspace scoping in the Rally UI affects WSAPI queries — Broadcom Knowledge
  7. Parent-Child Relationships — Broadcom TechDocs
  8. Managing Iterations — Broadcom TechDocs
  9. Close Projects and Workspaces — Broadcom TechDocs
  10. Advanced WSAPI Rally Topics — Broadcom TechDocs
  11. What are team-managed and company-managed projects? — Atlassian Support

Gaps & Limitations

  • WSAPI canonical Project entity reference. The Broadcom WSAPI overview and “Advanced WSAPI Topics” pages do not publish a flat field-by-field schema for the Project entity; the field-level facts here are stitched from the create-child-project page (Parent Team), the user-permissions API article (ProjectPermission), and the close-projects page (State). A canonical schema dump is only fully visible to authenticated tenants browsing the in-product Object Model dropdown.
  • Maximum Project nesting depth. Broadcom describes hierarchies as “multi-level” and gives no published numeric cap; this report cannot confirm whether a hard limit exists at the data-model level.
  • Permission cascade semantics. The fetched docs describe an opt-in “copy users from parent at create time” flow but do not document whether ongoing changes to the parent’s permissions propagate to children. Best inference: they do not (copy-at-creation only). Confirming this would require an authenticated test or a deeper KB search.
  • Cross-project work-item move. The close-projects page implies the Project field on a work item is editable (you can change it even on a locked Project), but a dedicated “move story between projects” documentation page was not surfaced in the fetched material.
  • Aha!’s direct workspace-types page redirected and was not re-fetched; coverage is from the search snippet plus the workspace-hierarchy support page. The split between “Workspace” and “Project Workspace type” should be cross-checked before publishing externally.
  • Linear Initiatives, Cycles are described at a conceptual level only; depth/nesting limits not investigated.
  • Jira Plans (formerly Advanced Roadmaps) mentioned but not deep-dived — behaviour relative to Project containers wasn’t verified.
